author: @pazguatos
text: Hi Robert! Great video and such an inspiring topic, thank you my friend and let me say your eyes and face shine beautifully and your voice hasn't lost your music in it, I want to suggest, since I'm trying something similar myself, a design based on internal cones for 3d printed hinges, distributing the weight evenly in a smooth (hence my headaches) surface, with two big parts that fit into each other in a shape resembling an egg, if you provide a way I can upload the 3mfs or stl documents, it's a work in progress tho and therefor unimportant, for now. I hope you enjoy the rest of the day, and thank you deeply for what you've done with your channel, I can never thank enough for what you gave to me (company when most needed) and to the world, unimaginable, you are both blessed and a blessing

author: @bigbadbith8422

author: @glencwilson
text: Have been playing with Tinkercad and getting absolutely frustrated with it. This video appeared in the suggestions. Learnt so much in 10 minutes it was annoying that I hadn't seen this channel before. Love the style of presentation. Thanks!

replies.author: @MisterkeTube
replies.text: And now you no longer get frustrated with Tinkercad?  I even get annoyed when I see how much time someone spends on a design like this which in something like Fusion or OnShape would take 1 sketch and a couple of extrudes, i.e. less than a minute AND allowing you to go back into the sketch and change the dimensions in seconds if you might have gotten those wrong (ex. the tolerances needed for your 3d printer).  And at least to me they also seem easier than all the clicks needed in Tinkercad to switch accuracies or align/move stuff and their circles are actually circles, not 64-corner-polygons ...  That said, this makes me even more admire those who make complex devices with Tinkercad.  I just wonder how much more those people could do when moving to a decent CAD program ...

replies.author: @glencwilson
replies.text: @MisterkeTube  Yes, there are still some frustrations but I find it easier to use than any of the 'proper' CAD applications I have tried. I have used design software for mainframe printing and was a mainframe analyst/programmer. Certainly not IT illiterate and can learn new things but not CAD it seems.

author: @crenshaw200
text:
Nice to discover ways of doing things easier for one self in TinkerCAD, thank you!
Made an adapter for a belt wheel (that came from a washing machine) that had an oval center hole, I filled the gap to center the axle and added five blocks to grab hold of the wheels structure and then put a spring pin through the adapter and axle.
That way, me and my brother have now built a two meter long motor driven soil trommel.

author: @williwonti
text: One good habit to get into with Tinkercad is to copy and paste the parts before and after you join them together to preserve them in case you need to tweak something later. Most of my Tinkercad files' perimeters are littered with all the different steps it took to make the final product

author: @brandonlaird6876
text: I think the hardest thing I made myself was a test screw / nut. Getting the nut to be a true hex, and getting the screw threading to be addable (add x mm heights where the threading continues without flat bits). I didn't need it to conform to an existing thread standard, only itself, I don't know if it conforms to any standard threading.

author: @ezone913
text:
I've hit the limits with Tinkercad and started to learn FreeCAD to build with more complex shapes.  It's not as difficult as I feared and it's a totally free program. 
Tinkercad is an outstanding teaching tool to get you comfortable building with simple shapes. FreeCAD opens so many new doors to create in.  
Great video, Robert.  Thanks for sharing.

author: @rogerwilson6367
text: I have used Solidworks for over 20 years as a design engineer, but I struggle with TinkerCad.

replies.author: @ericwazhung
replies.text:
Solidworks background, myself... (albeit, not much) I was admiring how quickly these sorts of parts can be one-offed with his techniques... I'd be trying to make everything scalable and constrained to the point of 3-4hours to come up with these three simple parts he did in 10min!
I wonder about e.g. throwing the hole through the cylinder on the same plane, are these systems now guaranteed not to give zero-thickness? Really, it doesnt even matter with 3Dprinting, could easily just pop it out. What a different way of thinking!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: parametric modelling does have another dimension - but tinkercad is a great teaching and learning tool - at this stage i find the ideas and methodology of 3d design often foxes people - which is why i bang on about that bit lol

replies.author: @rogerwilson6367
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I know what you mean!

author: @daneking1209
text: I’ve pleaded in the comment section several times about this. All these videos of power generation are pointless without a video of how to store this power. Where’s the video that shows people how to take power produced by the inventions and store it in a solar generator?

replies.author: @McRootbeer
replies.text: ... do you mean a battery?

replies.author: @daneking1209
replies.text: @McRootbeer If I build a ceiling fan wind turbine as an example how do I get that energy into the battery correctly. There’s a lot in between power generation an storage and that middle ground has gone completely undressed by this channel

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have made 168 videos on how to make batteries, supercapacitors and control and charging circuits - they are even in a playlist - where on earth are you looking?

replies.author: @daneking1209
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering thank you for your reply, I don’t need a battery. I have an Ecoflow. What would I search for on how to charge it using one of your power generators?
